question: What trend can be observed in the Price of cars with advanced safety features (more Airbags) across different Production years?

analysis.design_rationale: A heatmap allows for efficient visualization of the relationship between two quantitative variables (number of Airbags and Production year) and their effect on Price. By binning both 'Airbags' and 'Prod. year', we aggregate Price and reveal how combinations of safety features over time correspond to average car prices. This design scales well for large tables and gives an overview of how advanced safety features (more Airbags) are valued in newer models.
analysis.target_insight: Identifies which Production years and levels of Airbags have the highest average prices, revealing price trends for advanced-safety cars over time, and whether newer cars with more airbags are priced higher.

chart_encodings.other_aesthetics: Color gradient with value labels; grid lines

transformations: Filter df to only include rows with Airbags >= df['Airbags'].mean() to focus on advanced safety cars
Bin 'Airbags' into 5 quantile groups to reduce heatmap granularity
Bin 'Prod. year' into 6 equal-width intervals spanning the full range, to group by era
Group by the binned 'Airbags' and 'Prod. year', aggregate average Price per group
data_preparation_steps: Create Airbags bins using pd.qcut with 5 quantiles
Create Prod. year bins using pd.cut into 6 intervals
Group by Airbags_bin and Prod_year_bin, calculate mean of Price for each group
Pivot grouped data so rows are Airbags_bin, columns are Prod_year_bin, values are mean Price
suggestion.matplotlib_code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib as mpl
df = pd.read_csv("csv_c_squared\28.csv")
# Filter for advanced safety: Airbags >= mean
mean_airbags = df['Airbags'].mean()
df_adv = df[df['Airbags'] >= mean_airbags]
# Bin Airbags into 5 quantiles
airbags_bins = pd.qcut(df_adv['Airbags'], 5, duplicates='drop')
df_adv['Airbags_bin'] = airbags_bins.astype(str)
# Bin Prod. year into 6 equal-width intervals
prod_year_bins = pd.cut(df_adv['Prod. year'], 6)
df_adv['Prod_year_bin'] = prod_year_bins.astype(str)
# Group & aggregate
grouped = df_adv.groupby(['Airbags_bin', 'Prod_year_bin'])['Price'].mean().reset_index()
pivot = grouped.pivot(index='Airbags_bin', columns='Prod_year_bin', values='Price')
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 7))
cmap = mpl.colormaps['viridis']
im = ax.imshow(pivot.values, aspect='auto', cmap=cmap)
# Set ticks and labels
ax.set_xticks(np.arange(pivot.shape[1]))
ax.set_xticklabels(list(pivot.columns), rotation=45, ha='right')
ax.set_yticks(np.arange(pivot.shape[0]))
ax.set_yticklabels(list(pivot.index))
ax.set_xlabel('Production Year (binned)')
ax.set_ylabel('Number of Airbags (binned quantiles)')
# Annotate cells
for i in range(pivot.shape[0]):
    for j in range(pivot.shape[1]):
        val = pivot.values[i, j]
        if not np.isnan(val):
            ax.text(j, i, f"{val:.0f}", ha='center', va='center', color='white', fontsize=9)
plt.title('Average Price of Advanced-Safety Cars\nAcross Production Years and Airbag Levels')
cbar = fig.colorbar(im, ax=ax, orientation='vertical', label='Average Price')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
